Just to the east are two white-capped domes from which you’ll get better views of this colorful and imposing landscape. Below you, hoodoos, goblins, and columns rise from the eroded bluff, capped by harder layers of tuff. In spring, wildflowers bloom on the soft, sandy slope. From the top of the closest dome , you’ll get more views of all these formations and exposed bands of rock across the open canyon. On the east side of the Owyhee River are the black lava expanses of Lambert Rocks.

Continue on down to the lower dome . You’ll pass across a sere bench of scattered basalt blocks before scrambling to the broad summit. From here, you’ll get better views north up the Owyhee Canyon and the fanciful formations below Peak 3518 that enchant river rafters. Below the upper dome , you can see columns capped by a harder, darker layer, almost like a table cloth.

When you’ve had your fill of these exhilarating vistas, begin hiking west along the rim of the bowl. The soft sand here makes this a bit of a slog. Views open up north to the bluffs of Chalk Basin. Make for a sagebrush high point from which to orient. From this spot, you can see the low profile of Steens Mountain to the southwest. Then look northwest along the southern rim of Chalk Basin, and you should be able to spot your vehicle.

Make your way along the rim of Chalk Basin, keeping your vehicle in sight as much as possible. A leopard lizard or two might rustle off as you pass. You’ll cross two tumbleweed-jammed draws and pass to the right side of a knoll, staying high before winding through the sagebrush. The jeep track down into Chalk Basin appears to your right. You’ll descend into a last draw before reaching the plateau again and your car.
Trip Report 1 Text: (Last report from my car camping trip to the Owyhee.) These two destinations are off on narrow dirt tracks many miles from a highway. The roads were dry, so they wouldn’t have been much of a problem, when driven with care, for any but the lowest-slung vehicles. When it’s wet, however, it’s a different matter – there are several shallow draws to cross and no bridges. 1. Coffeepot Crater The crater is the only really accessible part of the Jordan Craters complex. The lava flow from Coffeepot Crater is the youngest in the area, at most 3,200 years old, and thus well within the period of human settlement. On the west side of the crater is a black cinder slope, while lava spilled through two breeches on the east side. I walked south along the edge of the lava flow a little way and then back along it, admiring the pahoehoe formations and the plants and lizards that have found a home here. On the northeast side of the crater, a lava runway issues down towards a couple of collapse pits. On the west side, a low ridge of maybe eight or nine spatter cones run along a fissure into the balsamroot hillside. 2. Chalk Basin On this one I assiduously followed William L. Sullivan’s directions in his Eastern Oregon guidebook. I camped the night here at Sullivan’s X-shaped junction. On the drive in, I saw two big clouds of dust and thought there must be others driving out, but they turned out to be two herds of mustangs cantering with the wind. On the drive back out, it was small groups of antelope that would race me (not such a challenge, since I was averaging about 20 mph) and then suddenly turn across the road. The next day, I hiked down into Chalk Basin, a large sagebrush bowl with tuff cliffs that look something like the Pillars of Rome, but without the pillars. Down by a wash, there were the remains of a teardrop trailer and a bed, perhaps some old trapper’s retreat when out here harvesting coyotes during the winter. [Edit: Actually probably from the Desert Sunrise claim of the 1980s, which gathered banded "decorative rock" from the area.] Sullivan tells you to walk down the wash, which was running water in places, until you get to the top of a substantial dry waterfall. I then took a ridge up and over to a tributary wash and followed this out of Chalk Basin to a rim overlooking an unnamed canyon that opens up to the Owyhee River across from Lambert Rocks. This is the real destination of this cross-country loop. There are two white-topped domes on the rim that offer views down and across to an array of hoodoos and columns, many capped by a hard thin tuff layer. From the lower dome, you can see more formations to the north above the river itself. A marvelous vista and, like all my outings in the Owyhee except for those in Leslie Gulch, I had it to myself. Then it was a question of hiking back to my car, which I could see from a high point, across the sagebrush. Steens Mountain was low on the horizon and still displaying substantial snow. A couple of leopard lizards froze in portrait pose. The day was still quite young when I, with a little regret that I hadn’t planned a longer trip, motored back to the highway.
Trip Report 2 Text: We took our first trip down to SE Oregon last week. We're slowly working on checking off as many of Sullivan's featured hikes as we can so we started our trip by driving from Salem to Bend via Biggs Junction to hike the Deschutes River Trail at Deschutes Park. I know that's a long way from SE Oregon but it did involve sagebrush . We took the River Trail to Gordon Canyon and then the Ferry Springs Trail on the way back. After a night in Bend we drove to Burns and headed down to the Malheur Wildlife Refuge.  We took the short Overlook Trail down to the Park HQ.  The museum there was great. After speaking with one of the volunteers who gave us some suggestions for our visit we did a portion of the driving tour to the Buena Vista Trail (a short trail up to an overlook) then stopped at the Diamond Craters Outstanding Natural Area and the Peter French Round Barn before ending up in Caldwell, ID for the night. The third day we hiked at Jordan Craters then drove to Leslie Gulch and hiked in Slocum, Timber, Juniper and Dago Gulches. We stayed in Caldwell again that night then the next morning did the short .7 mile RT hike to Jump Creek Falls in Idaho before driving down to Three Forks and hiking along the Owyhee River. We stayed in Jordan Valley at the Basque Station Motel that night and in the morning visited the Pillars of Rome (just outside of Rome, OR) then drove the horrible dirt (and rock) road to Chalk Basin for a spectacular off trail hike there. Pillars of Rome From Chalk Basin we drove to Fields Station via the East Steens Road where we stayed the night after having one their bacon cheeseburgers and milkshakes.  On the drive we spotted a couple of sandhill cranes. The following day we did three shorter hikes at Borax Hot Springs, Big Sand Gap, and Pike Creek before ending the day in Burns. Somewhere along the way we picked up a small nail which the good folks at the Les Schwab in Burns patched up for us. We did our longest hike of the trip the next day with a 14.2 mile out and back on the Myrtle Creek Trail 35 miles north of Burns in the Malheur National Forest. After Myrtle Creek we stayed in Bend again and visited the Cove Palisades State Park the next morning before returning to Salem. [/url] Unbelievably we didn't see a single rattlesnake the whole trip, just a lone garter snake at Chalk Basin.  We were lucky enough to see a badger on our drive back to East Steens Road from Borax Lake but it didn't stick around long enough for me to get a picture. The trip involved a lot of driving and the roads to Three Forks and Chalk Basin were ugly in spots and require high clearance vehicles.  We walked the final mile plus of road for the Jordan Craters, Three Forks and Chalk Basin hikes. The road to Pike Creek was also in pretty bad shape. As for other hikers the only ones we passed were on the Deschutes River Trails and at Juniper Gulch but we do tend to get early starts and I am sure if it hadn't been 6am when we were at Jump Creek Falls we would have run into people there at least.

Description:
Riparian habitat is being restored along the Chehalis River north of a 90-degree bend west of Centralia in a farming area called Fords Prairie. The land here, near Centralia’s wastewater treatment plant, belongs to the city but is being restored and maintained by the Chehalis River Basin Land Trust. Parts of the landscape may be flooded in winter, so you probably want to avoid the trail for a few days after heavy rains. This is a flat walk, but there’s a chance to spot local wildlife any time of the year. Note that the trailhead has been moved away from the end of Goodrich Road to a parking area at the wastewater treatment plant itself.

From the parking area, walk towards the river past the barn-like buildings of Centralia’s wastewater treatment plant. Rejoin Goodrich Road, and continue west past farm fields to the old parking area, now gated and blocked, and the beginning of the trail. Somewhat intimidating signage on private property in the vicinity is a defense against some of the activity that ensued at the parking area after the trail was opened in 2006, including prostitution and drug dealing (this is why the trailhead was moved back). The wide gravel trail swings right and then left past farm fields and reaches a barn where you can look up to see the mud nests of cliff swallows under the eastern eaves. Past the barn, there’s a covered shelter, a shed, and then a colorful sign depicting the trail near a tall Norway spruce. You can pick up a guide for the numbered interpretive hike here.

Make a right at a T-junction , and hike north on a grassy road track under tall black cottonwoods with the Chehalis River flowing lazily to your left. Riverbank vegetation includes red alder, red osier dogwood, willow, and also invasive Armenian blackberry. Signs warn you to keep back from the unstable river bank. The river, flowing north here, braids around a large gravel bar. Keep an eye out for the resident bald eagle pair that often perch in the cottonwoods on the west bank. Close to the trail, you may also spot beaver gnawings. Pass a rather surreal vent for an effluent pipe from the wastewater treatment plant. After walking by another gnarly old cottonwood, you’ll hike under powerlines as the tread becomes increasingly humped with mole hills. The farm fields to the right, owned by the City of Centralia, are flooded most winters. The track continues past a riparian thicket dominated by big-leaf maples, and you’ll reach the end of the North Trail opposite a forested bluff. (The interpretive guide tells you that the land here was formed under the ocean, and the river’s erosion of the bluff has exposed numerous marine fossils.)

Return to the T-junction near the barn, and keep walking south on the South Trail. There’s a restored thicket of riparian vegetation to the right and a farm field owned by the city to the left. Keep straight past Post #22, and pass along the verge of the field. After you enter a grove of Douglas-fir and western red-cedar, stay left at a junction and follow the trail around to a 90-degree bend in the Chehalis River. Here the river, flowing from the east, butted against the forested hill on the opposite bank and took a turn north. South of the bend, you can see a swampy eddy at the confluence with Lincoln Creek : this area was once used as a log pond by a sawmill. The trail drops to a beach, where you can spot for migrating salmon and the dipping flight of kingfishers.

Return to the junction at Post #23, and make a left to reach the field and hike out to your vehicle.

Hike Name: Christmas Valley Sand Dunes Loop Hike
Description:
The largest spread of inland dunes in the Pacific Northwest is about 23 miles from Christmas Valley in northern Lake County. The dunes, some of them 60 feet high, are on Bureau of Land Management land and are mostly composed of ash blown east from the eruption of Mount Mazama, now Crater Lake , about 7,000 years ago. Dust from local playa lakes, such as nearby Fossil Lake, has added to the dunes. There are no trails in this remote area, but a large area of the dunes is a playground for ATVers. The best times for hikers to come here are the spring and the fall, in cooler weather, and with a negligible ATV presence, especially on weekdays. The short loop below is suggested, but obviously you can travel in any direction you want!

From the parking spot, head straight towards the dunes on a sandy track to reach a Y-junction. Bear right, and continue walking through a landscape of western juniper, sagebrush, and rabbitbrush. Head into the sand to crest the first dunes. You’ll see that small playas, paved with hexagonal hardpan patterns, have been windswept  clear of sand between the dunes. To the west, you’ll see taller dunes, so head in that direction.

You’ll cross a grassy flat and notice a rather gnarly old juniper atop a nearby dune. Head towards this tree and climb the dune to walk its crest, the slope steep on the windward side and less so on the lee. Look for beetle, lizard, and mouse tracks. To the south and west, there is just a sea of sand dunes, while to the north, shrub islands and individual pines and junipers survive in the sand. When you see the blue port-a-potty at Juniper Camp, you can turn north to reach the sagebrush steppe.

Here, you’ll find a track where you can turn right. On this hard pan, walking is easier. A few ponderosa pines exist here, and flowering plants like milkvetch and buckwheat find footing. There’s a ferruginous hawk nest in the vicinity, so you may get angry passovers from a parent. The track will take you back directly to your vehicle.
Trip Report 1 Text: I spent a couple of days visiting (mostly revisiting) various spots south of Bend and out to Christmas Valley. Christmas Valley Sand Dunes This is the largest area of inland dunes in the Pacific Northwest. I did a loop from near East Camp across a few dunes and then back along the foot of the dunes. ATVs are permitted to run here, but none were about when I was slogging through the sand. Out of the dunes, I was buzzed by irate ferruginous hawks defending their nest. Eventually they settled in separate trees and gave me the gory eyeball until I left their territory. A western kingbird watched the commotion with some amusement. Sand Rock (Lost Forest) I scrambled up and around this formation in the Lost Forest. Sand Rock is tuff, not sand, and is honeycombed by chemical weathering. From the top there are views to the Newberry Caldera, the Cascades, Hager Mountain, and Yamsay Mountain. I crossed over the rock and skittered down a sand slide to loop back to my car through a portion of the Lost Forest. These ponderosas survive on half the amount of normal rainfall by reaching through the porous sandy soil to underground water trapped on the impervious bed of an ancient lake. Crack in the Ground The whole crack is about two miles long and can be explored in various segments, some necessitating short scrambles. It was created when the neighboring Four Craters lava flow, through its sheer weight, depressed the land, causing it to separate slightly and form a fissure at the western edge of the depression. A trail leads from the parking area to the first, and deepest, section. It’s like being in a cave, markedly cooler than the surrounding steppe, and plants like nettles and sedges survive down there. After the first segment, I hiked above or entered other segments. At the fourth section, I was greeted by a raucous chirping, obviously hungry chicks badgering a parent. I decided to back out of the crack to give them some space, and hiked along the rim until I saw the telltale white splashes of uric acid. I never saw the chicks, which were tucked under a rock overhang, but mom soon emerged, soared high in the sky, and then came at me in a steep dive. It was a female prairie falcon, and I could feel her slipstream as she buzzed me. After a few passes like that, she settled on a juniper and awaited my withdrawal. Derrick Cave Fail The old way to Derrick Cave and the northeast corner of the Devil’s Garden was from the Fort Rock Guard Station. Using old information, I went this way first (the way I had gone in the past), and soon encountered a closed road sign at a cattle grid. Derrick Cave Road runs across private land at this point, and the owners have closed it off. The current way to go is from Fort Rock, using Cabin Lake Road and then a series of forest roads north of the Devil’s Garden. I got just past Cabin Lake and reached a barricade. The Forest Service has closed off a whole stretch of forest until 2026 for “fire mitigation” and restoration. Hole in the Ground This is a volcanic crater west of Fort Rock reached by a rough forest road. I parked on the rim and walked down into the crater, which is a mile wide, via an ATV track. There’s a ponderosa wood on the southern slope of the crater and a few ponderosas studded about the wide open crater floor. The crater was created by violent eruptions when magma reached reservoirs of groundwater, thus initiating a steam explosion. On the sagebrush floor of the crater, various wildflowers were blooming. I took a very steep ATV track up the other side and circled back around the rim. Lava Butte I hit this one in the late afternoon, Usually, you have to take a shuttle bus from the Lava Lands Visitor Center, but I was able to drive up. There’s a very active lookout at the summit and a half mile trail around the cinder cone. The volcano produced the flow of black lava that oozed three miles to the Deschutes, damming it for several hundred years and creating Benham Falls. The Molten Land This interpretive trail leads out of the visitor center, with signs along the way explaining the volcanics and other natural history. Basically, you’re walking across Lava Butte’s contorted landscape of back lava where sagebrush lizards bask and a few plants, such as ponderosa pines, penstemon, lupine, and buckwheat, survive in small pockets of soil. The trail ends at a viewpoint towards the central Cascades. Paulina Creek Falls I went up to Paulina Lake and was hoping to explore features on the caldera rim, but there was still three feet of snow not far from the lake, so I walked both sides of Paulina Creek Falls. The creek braids above the drop, so this is a twin falls. About 2,000 years ago, an eruption tripped a massive flood which, at 10,000 cubic feet per second, gouged the creek’s gorge in just a few hours. There are three viewpoints here: one at the base of the falls, another up top on the south side of the gorge, and a third on the opposite rim. While crossing the road bridge, I encountered a dark creature making its way to the lake. I only got a fuzzy photo, but the otter had recently been in a fight and had some exposed wounds on its head, including a missing ear. McKay Crossing Falls I began this hike at the western end of the Peter Skene Ogden Trail and followed Paulina Creek up to the falls, which are just below McKay Crossing Campground. The creek tumbles and splashes its course through the ponderosa woods, with a narrow strip of verdancy next to the water.

Description:
Like the Bureau of Land Management’s Whittaker Creek Recreation Area , the same agency’s Clay Creek Recreation Site offers camping, day use facilities and a short loop trail that leads up a ridge of old-growth Douglas-firs. The trails at Whittaker Creek and Clay Creek were both constructed in 1991, with the Clay Creek Trail recently refurbished and restored to its original loop layout.

From the parking pullout, walk 50 yards down the road to a trailhead sign and map fashioned by a Cole Spring Eagle Scout troop. You’ll immediately cross a footbridge over Clay Creek, which runs under rustling red alders, big-leaf maples, and drooping salmonberry bushes. A trail register post no longer sports a register, so continue to the right to pass your first big Douglas-fir. You’ll also get a glimpse of the confluence of Clay Creek with the Siuslaw River. The trail switchbacks up twice in a mixed forest of big-leaf maple, vine maple, Douglas-fir, western hemlock, and western red-cedar. You’ll pass through a cutting in a massive fallen Douglas-fir and then through the same tree again where it’s six feet in diameter. A few steps set in the trail take you up to the loop junction, where you can keep left.

The trail immediately switchbacks up twice and makes a traverse in more open woods with some madrones and spring-blooming iris. Four more switchbacks take you up to a large bench on the ridge crest. From here, you’ll hike up to the high point of the hike, where there’s another bench under a big Douglas-fir. The trail then descends past more large Douglas-firs and also a few madrones before switchbacking off the ridge to reach the loop junction, where you’ll keep left to descend to the trailhead.
Trip Report 1 Text: On a brief excursion to the area, I did these three hikes, two of them very short, in the central part of Oregon’s Coast Range. 1. Cummins Ridge Trail (Cummins Creek Wilderness) This six-mile trail bisects the Cummins Creek Wilderness from west to east, and it’s an easy lope on a gentle rolling ridge. The first half of the route is along an overgrown forest road, while the upper half follows a trail that ends at a trailhead at 2,175 feet above sea level. The ridge forest here was probably more diverse before Euro-American settlers arrived, but is now almost all Douglas-fir up to 150 years in age. Much larger rotting snags in the forest attest to the results of the devastating Great Yaquina Fire of 1849, one of a series of settler-caused fires fanned by September east winds that absolutely incinerated most coastal forest from Tillamook Bay to the Siuslaw River between 1845 and 1853. It is instructive to ponder the effects of such conflagrations as you hike along the ridge. Now that it’s protected, in another 100 years or so, it will be real old growth. By the way, many online references to this trail talk about “towering Sitka spruce” on the trail. There are few spruce, all close to the lower trailhead. If you want to see old growth Sitka Spruce, they’re down by Cummins Creek. There’s a user path that splits off the Cummins Creek Trail (not in the wilderness); it enters the wilderness and heads to the creek, where you’ll find a number of huge spruce trees. 2. Wasson Lake (Devils Staircase Wilderness) The Devils Staircase Wilderness, created in 2019, is our most recent wilderness. I had intended to do the semi-bushwhack down to the famed Devils Staircase, but the Wasson Ridge Road which carries you the final five or six miles to the trailhead was a clutter of salmonberry. Bulldozing my 2019 Subaru through that was, I knew, a divorceable offense, so I sought out little Wasson Lake, also in the wilderness. I found a pullout and began flailing around under big trees in a dense understory until I spotted the lake, soon after which I came across a bona fide trail that led to a campsite right on the lake, which is within the wilderness. It’s a still, dark, quiet body of water near the headwaters of Wasson Creek, which a few miles downstream splashes over the Devils Staircase. Like most Coast Range lakes, it was formed either by a slide or a beaver dam, and probably within the last 200 years given the dead snags jutting eerily from its waters. The short trail led back out only 0.2 miles to Wasson Lake Road and a second pullout only 150 yards from the one I chose to bushwhack from. A couple of new signs in the area announce the new wilderness although they’re both a little distance from the actual boundary. One is at Hell Pond, just off Wasson Lake Road, and the other was at the junction of Ferntop and Wells Creek Roads. 3. Clay Creek Loop (BLM) The BLM prides itself on a couple of small stands of old-growth Douglas-fir that it has preserved among its extensive holdings in the central Coast Range. This short loop trail was refurbished in the last few years and takes you 500 feet up a ridge above the Siuslaw River. It’s a rather out of the way destination, part of the Clay Creek Recreation Site, but worthy of a visit if you happen to be in the area.

Description:
Coffenbury Lake , part of Fort Stevens State Park, is one of many shallow interdune lakes on the Clatsop Plains, a sandy and now vegetated expanse formed by deposition from the Columbia River. Even though there are no inflowing or outflowing streams, the water level fluctuates as much as six feet with changes in the water table. The short loop hike is very close to the park's campground and is recommended for families with smaller children. There is even a summer swimming area! For those with time and energy, Coffenbury Lake can also be visited as part of the Fort Stevens Loop Hike .

Walk below the restrooms and through the picnic area. Head right along the shore and a small sandy beach to pick up the footpath leading up the alder-lined west shore of the lake. The path rises under Sitka spruce with evergreen huckleberry, sword fern, salal and holly in the undergrowth. Then the trail drops and keeps along a shoreline shaded by cedars and shore pines. Reach some larger spruces and the marshy south end of the lake.

At a trail junction, go left on a vegetated causeway with more wetland on the right (Spur trails here connect with the private trails of OMSI's Camp Kiwanilong). The trail runs close to the eastern shore now. Look for waterfowl, including flotillas of buffleheads. You're also likely to see mallards and cormorants. Pass through a grassy picnic area and keep to the shore to pick up the trail again. In spring, you might enjoy the odd sight of steelhead wallowing in the shallows: these are surplus hatchery fish that have been released here. Eventually, reach the Coffenbury Lake Trailhead and your vehicle.

The Columbia Children’s Arboretum, a Portland City Park, stands on land originally owned by the old Columbia School District and marked as a possible site for a new high school in the 1920s. The area was taken in by Portland Public Schools in 1964. Students from the local middle school, which served a lower income and very transient neighborhood, began working here under the GROW program, part of a hands-on approach to science. The brush was cleared, orchards, trees, and organic gardens planted, and the 50 state trees project was begun. However, in 1983, the Columbia Middle School was closed and the interest in the park dwindled as the site became a magnet for homeless campers. Portland Parks purchased the acreage from the public schools in 1999 and began a rehabilitation effort with the help of local volunteers. Many of the state trees have been replanted, and new signage has been posted. This quiet haven is now an inviting enclave for tree lovers and school groups among the industrial expanses of North Portland.

Walk up the paved and bollarded road from the parking area. There’s an alder-shaded ditch to your left. The closed road bends right past spruce and beech trees and an old orchard. You'll reach a port-a-potty, kiosk, and a couple of ADA parking spots at a vehicle turnaround. There's an expanse of lawn beyond the orchard, and a path leading left crosses a footbridge to Meadow Drive. Stay on the main path to cross the Towle Bridge, named after two of the park’s benefactors, which crosses the moat, or drainage ditch, that encircles the main part of the park. Willow, cottonwood, and alder shade this quiet backwater. You can look also for beaver activity here.

At a Japanese red pine, a Portland Heritage Tree, you can make out a chip path leading right. You'll pass a couple of longleaf pines, the state tree of North Carolina: this western area of the park is where the state trees are. You’ll now begin to circle the long meadow in the middle of the park and can make diversions to some of the labeled exhibits. For example, to your left are a white birch (New Hampshire) and an eastern cottonwood (Nebraska). The trail enters the thickets above the moat, where a more natural cover of Pacific Northwest vegetation - Douglas-fir, Pacific willow, black cottonwood, red osier dogwood, and high bush cranberry - predominates. You'll duck through a willow tunnel and emerge at the meadow. Keep right at a junction where a trail leads to the small rose garden and a grove of cherries. Then, pass a path that leads out to Lija Loop. Circle around on grass past a blackberry thicket to reach the main paved path through the arboretum. The other park trailhead, at Meadow Lane, is to your right.

Turn left to walk past a kiosk and port-a-potty. Then there's a tall row of maples and American gums, which offer a spectacular fall display. You'll continue on the main trail under maples and sycamores, passing the park's picnic area. A bench invites rest at a semicircle of native plants: snowberry, Oregon grape, and ninebark. In a more open area, there are a few sugar maples. Past these, note the eastern white pines and oaks. Then recross the Towle Bridge, and head back to your car.
Trip Report 1 Text: During the fall, which is just nearing its end, I took a number of spins around the Metro area working on an accessibility project. This is a sort of random tour of some of the sites I visited. Tonquin Villebois Loop The Ice Age Tonquin Trail is a partially completed set of inter-urban trails mainly involving the cities of Wilsonville, Tualatin, and Sherwood and unincorporated spaces in-between. The trails lead through a landscape of rather subtle formations resulting from the Missoula Floods of 17,000 - 12,000 years ago, when the Willamette Valley saw multiple back flows from the Columbia of the floods up to 400 feet deep; these created the temporary Lake Allison. Tonquin, known to geologists as the Tonquin Scablands, refers to an area in the middle of the three cities where there are several quarries that mine the flood deposits. This walk begins in Graham Oaks Nature Park and passes through the modern Villebois neighborhood of Wilsonville, once the site of the Dammasch State Hospital, a psychiatric institution in the style of traditional “insane asylums”, which was closed down in 1995. The trail is planned to extend across the Coffee Creek Wetlands but this section is only on the drawing board, so I cut south to another section of the trail in the Morey’s Landing area and then back to Graham Oaks. Hayden Bay A paved trail runs from the Holiday Inn through several condo complexes (public welcome; restrictions apply) on the south bank of the Columbia, passing the marina on Hayden Bay. During Covid, an HOA at the east end of the route gated their section, so I looped back past little Lotus Isle Park, which once was the site of Portland’s largest amusement park. The complex was destroyed in 1932 by the antics of an enraged elephant named Tusko. You can still see the pilings of the streetcar trestle that led here in North Portland Harbor. Columbia Children’s Arboretum This acreage, which had once been a school project but languished and turned into a transient camping area, was reinvigorated beginning in 1999. There’s a collection of state trees, nicely labeled. You can make a little loop along a drainage ditch and a paved trail that runs through the developed area of the park. Whitaker Ponds The two ponds are part of the Columbia Slough wetland complex in industrial North Portland. This was another 21st century rehabilitation project, and a loop leads around the West Pond. There’s also a boardwalk that accesses the shore. A proper parking area was recently constructed. Ch’ak Ch’ak Trail New sections of the 40-mile loop were opened in September, the route now known at the Ch’ak Ch’ak Trail ( ch’ak ch’ak is Chinook Wawa for bald eagle). I walked from both ends, first starting at Depot Park in Troutdale. This old railroad station is now a visitor’s center but also houses a small railroad museum. A trail leads under the Sandy River railroad bridge and across a sort of wasteland that serves as a disc golf course to reach the new Ch’ak Ch’ak Trail just before the I-84 bridge over the Sandy. The paved trail then joins the dike path that curves north and west. From the west end, I began at Chinook Landing, and followed the path along the dike as far as Company Lake. You can drop from here into a cottonwood bottomland to reach Sundial Beach on the Columbia. Bird Alliance of Oregon This is the former Audubon Society of Portland. There have been some trail revisions here, with some routes being decommissioned leaving essentially a short loop around the Pittock Sanctuary across Balch Creek. On the other side of Cornell, there are two loops, but access to the Collins Loop has been closed for some time because of an unsafe footbridge. You can access the Collins trails via the Founders Trail. Jenkins Estate There are pockets of woodland served by trails on this former country estate managed by Tualatin Hills Park and Recreation. The Jenkins house as well as the original house, now called the Gate House, are still there. In the developed part of the park, there are a Japanese Garden, rhododendron garden, herb garden, and education garden. Of particular note is the 100-year-old avenue of elms. Chehalem Ridge This Metro property is has matured since its opening three years ago. Other than the central gravel road, trails are well-graded and meandering. It is mostly plantation woodland that will be managed to grow more naturally. There are some views to the Coast Range from grassy Iowa Hill, where an oak savanna restoration is taking place. Rock Creek Greenway Eight miles in and out, this is a paved greenway under power lines. At the east end, the ambience is not so prepossessing as you pass soccer fields and a golf course. At Bethany Lake, where Springville Creek meets Rock Creek, you’ll begin spotting wetland wildlife. I spent some time at a marsh on Springville Creek watching egrets and waterfowl. Towards the east end of the corridor, you can make a short loop through the Kaiser Woods. Terwilliger to Willamette Park Loop From George Himes park, a trail takes you down to Iowa Street in South Portland. I took sidewalks to Willamette Park and walked the riverside greenway under turning cottonwoods. Then I headed up Taylors Ferry and took a narrow alley with 102 steps, eventually ending up at Fulton Park and crossing Barbur Boulevard. There are great views of Mt. Hood from California Street, and I closed the loop by walking the Terwilliger Pathway back to George Himes Park. Johnson Creek This route began in little Johnson Creek Park where Crystal Springs Creek joins Johnson Creek. From here, I joined the Springwater Corridor and followed it east over Johnson Creek, McLoughlin Boulevard, and the railroad. Past a deep cutting, I took the first diversion on an old, lumpy paved route that passes the abandoned tennis courts of the closed Eastmoreland Racquet Club. Back on the Springwater after recrossing Johnson Creek, I took a diversion again into the Tideman Johnson Natural Area and walked its boardwalk. The last diversion next to the Corridor was to the stone-walled observation deck over the 1930s WPA project that cut a canal across an oxbow bend in Johnson Creek to try and curtail destructive flooding. The WPA also created a small artificial waterfall here, but the project actually made the flooding problem worse. I also visited the other observation deck at the end of Harney Street, but some of these structures are going to disappear soon when a proposed City plan to "improve" high water flows takes effect. Lastly, I went up 45th Avenue and did a loop around Errol Heights Park, which has seen major makeover recently. A reconstructed wetland trail leads up a bluff to Tenino Court, which I then used to reach the upper area of the park. This has several nature play areas and play structures. A raised metal walkway switchbacks down to join the wetland trail.

Description:
Cook Hill , just to the east of Dog Mountain , serves as the latter’s poor cousin, but if you want solitude and an abundance of wildflower species in the spring, this is the place to go to avoid Dog’s crowds although it cannot quite replicate the spectacular meadow displays. The hike is all on unofficial trails and old road beds on public land save for a section of the ridge at the Cook Hill Saddle , which belongs to Broughton Lumber. There are two meadows to cross, a steep smaller meadow and the lengthy summit meadow, which has seen some grazing in the past. While all of the slopes were logged sometime in the 20th century, the forest wildflowers match those of the meadows in abundance and diversity. Note that the trail section which leads to the summit, constructed by Gorge activist Russ Jolley, can disappear in places as new growth engulfs it in the spring. The first time you're here, hike the loop counterclockwise as described to avoid the chance of losing Russ Jolley’s trail. When you return, you can go clockwise to get full frontal views of the Gorge as you descend from the summit of Cook Hill . There is no signage, but the routes are cleared by volunteers, usually every year.

Cross Cook-Underwood Road and walk west on Jackson Road 0.2 miles below a hillside of oaks and grassy meadows. Past the End County Road sign, the pavement ends: keep straight to a fork, where you go right into the woods past an open gate with a No Trespassing sign that you can ignore. Note the piles of junk on your right. Switchback and exit the Douglas-fir forest to cross a grassy slope above a water tank. Views open up to the Columbia River and the Oregon side of the Gorge. Switchback again to get more views and then enter a mixed woodland of Douglas-fir and big-leaf maple. After a third switchback, head up more steeply and swing left. The road bed heads up above a steep gully with a rushing stream. At a break in the trees, go left to view a small waterfall and cascade. Cross the creek – at this point you have risen above the worst of the poison oak although you wil notice a few little plants farther up. A trail detours from the road at a fallen tree. Back on the road track, keep rising in a dense Douglas-fir plantation below a talus alope. Cross the creek again and reach the Cook Hill Saddle-Cook Hill Summit Trail Junction , which has a small cairn.

Keep straight (right) here to pass above a small spring and walk on a long-abandoned road track studded with 40-50 year-old Douglas-firs. The verges of the path are carpeted with violets, candy flower, and Dutchman’s breeches. In a patch of cow parsnip, which will become towering plants in the summer, go right to leave the road bed and shortly reach another road bed. Go right here and soon head up the slope, make a traverse, and reach Cook Hill ’s steep lower meadow. Get a spectacular view across to Mount Defiance , Starvation Creek, and east to the Hood River valley. Balsamroot, buttercup, and lupine bloom in the meadow mid-spring. Cross the meadow by angling up to the left and finding the trail where it reenters the woods at the top of the meadow. Keep rising less steeply in a carpet of Solomon plume. Make a traverse through a thicket of vine maple; after this, angle up to get glimpses of Dog Mountain through the trees. Enter a small meadow, where the trail gets lost momentarily. Head up to the right between two Douglas-firs and reenter coniferous forest for a short spell. Emerge at Cook Hill ’s extensive summit meadow, which blooms with prairie violet, prairie star, larkspur, and sedge in the spring. Unlike the lower meadow, this meadow was grazed until the year 2000. Get views to Dog Mountain , Mount Defiance , and Mount Hood . Keep to the meadow, passing through a gap in a line of Douglas-firs and angle up to the right. Stay below the trees that stand along the summit ridge. The meadow gets rockier: this area becomes a vast display of grass widows in early spring. Near the top of the ridge, look for a small gap in the trees which will take you in to the ridge crest and the viewless summit.

From here, walk down the ridge and emerge at the meadow. Drop down steeply and then angle to the right to enter a young Douglas-fir plantation. Reach a road bed that leads along the ridge crest on a carpet of wild strawberry. You can get good views of Mount Adams from a couple of places here. Reach the first communication tower and walk around it to the left. Finally, attain the open saddle area, which is crossed by a wide powerline corridor and has two more radio facilities on its north end. This is the end of the Bunker Keys Road and the Cook Hill Saddle . Look west to Wind Mountain , Table Mountain , and other Gorge peaks; the view east is dominated by Mount Adams .

Look down to your left to see a road heading back into the forest. Where the road forks, keep right and descend through a vine maple thicket. At the next junction, keep right again and continue to descend. As you go down, there are a couple of small detours around blowdown. Pass a slope of big-leaf maples on your left and then emerge from the forest to cross a wide talus slope dotted with gnarly old trees. After reentering the forest, keep left at a grassy landing and descend to reach the Cook Hill Saddle-Cook Hill Summit Trail Junction , where you go right to return to Jackson Road.
Trip Report 1 Text: The flower display on Cook Hill must be near its peak.  The lower meadow has a beautiful display of balsam root with a few other varieties mixed.  The forest along the Russ Jolley loop also has a nice display with large patches of vanilla leaf along the way.  The upper meadow has at least a dozen varieties in bloom.  Past the summit you'll see displays of red currant, paintbrush, and a ridiculous number of strawberry blossoms.  Unfortunately, most of my photos were corrupted and are unusable. The trail is in good shape, although the upper road could use a little brushing out.  There are a few trees down on the road but nothing that you can't go under or around. I was the first person in the parking area at 0710.  By the time I returned at noon, there were 20+ cars in the lot and along the shoulder.  I only saw 3 people as I climbed the hill, all of whom passed me.  I encountered 21 people and 5 dogs as I descended. There was no sign of animals other than a relatively fresh pile of bear scat.  Birds were fairly active in the trees but I rarely saw them.  I haven't found any ticks either. Here are a few of my surviving photos in no particular order.
Trip Report 2 Text: My wife and I spent 5 hours on the trails and lounging in the meadows of Cook Hill.  Every time I start the summer season with this hike, I always end up wondering what I was thinking.  We enjoy going there but it is tough on the knees.  We pulled into the parking area around 8:45 AM.  There were 6 other cars there.  We saw maybe 12 people the entire day, and only very quickly in passing.  We had the upper meadow entirely to ourselves for about 25 minutes, something that would never happen on Dog Mountain on a sunny day in May. There was a diverse display of flowers including desert parsley, grass widows, paintbrush, waterleaf, lupine, and calypso orchids. There is a nice bloom of balsamroot in the lower meadow.  Trillium is in bloom along the trail from the lower meadow to the upper ones.  The upper meadows featured a range of small flowers with patches of glacier lily along the tree lines.  Most of the balsam root plants on the upper meadow need a few more days to a week to bloom. Trails conditions aren't bad but they could be better.  The old logging road that runs from the bottom to the BPA right of way needs a thorough brushing out of the upper 1-2 miles.  Some stretches have quite a bit of debris and there are some deadfalls to climb over or walk around.  There has been some tree clearing along lower part of the road just above the oak meadow at the bottom. I forgot about the poison oak.  It is leafing out along the first 1/4 of trail.  Some is starting to grow in the middle of the trail in the oak meadow at the bottom.  I definitely would not recommend letting a pet roam off leash like one party was doing. View from the lower meadow View from the upper meadow

Description:
The 11¾ mile Lower Rogue River Trail #1168 runs east from the community of Agness. It is a historic trail, once the only route for prospectors and upriver homesteaders to reach the coast. In the early 20th century, the trail was widened to four feet to accommodate narrow-gauge carts. When vehicle roads were constructed up the river, the route was abandoned but then restored as a trail in 1976. You’ll see traces of the old cart route along the way. The trail is also open to mountain bikers, but as of 2023, you can only explore it from one end or the other. In the spring of 2021, a slide obliterated a cliff section of the trail in Copper Canyon, and there’s no safe way to continue. It had been a few years since this trail, the poor sister to the popular Rogue River route between Grave Creek and Illahe, received much maintenance. The leaf litter lay thick on the tread, cows churned up the section above Big Eddy , and the footbridge was closed at Morris Rodgers Creek. However, TKO began to work on the route again in 2024.

Reaching the beginning of the trail is an adventure in itself. The local public library (a former school) and day park, in addition to the adjacent community center, are the only places you can park. From the library and day park, walk out the gate and turn right to head down Cougar Lane, passing the Lucas Pioneer Ranch and the community center. Here you’ll see a signboard for the Lower Rogue River Trail - you should scan it for any trail updates. Cougar Lane becomes a smooth gravel track that passes between two gated driveways before descending to cross Rilea Creek. Past the creek, and before a red barn, a trail sign points you up into the woods.

Follow the faint track here to circumvent private property, and then rejoin the road at a creek. Head up to the right again at a private gate, and enter a woodland of Douglas-fir, Oregon myrtle, oak, madrone, incense-cedar, and tanoak. The trail switchbacks down to an overgrown roadbed and crosses a footbridge over a ravine. You’ll catch a few views to the Rogue here. After a short rise, the trail drops across a steep, rushing creek under a spreading myrtle tree. Invasive blackberry is common at this end of the trail, and soon you’ll encounter a tangled mass of fallen trees. This necessitates a scramble down and along the rocky bank of the Rogue before an ascent through more blackberries. After a traverse, a trail junction invites you to drop down to the left to hike along the grassy benches above the cobbled bar at Big Eddy .

Cattle do graze here, but you can continue along the bench to the far end, where cow trails will take you back up into a myrtle grove and the Lower Rogue River Trail. The trail leads up a steep slope and reaches a private road, which you’ll follow down to the left a hundred yards to cross Blue Jay Creek on a wood vehicle bridge. Then you’ll pass through a gate to resume the trail above a private inholding. The trail briefly joins two old road beds and crosses a couple of small creeks. After this, you’ll wind up to reach the footbridge over Smithers Creek .

The trail splits 15 yards beyond the creek at an unsigned junction. Staying right will keep you on the Lower Rogue River Trail. Heading left will take you down to a view of the remains of a homestead above the river (this is now Forest Service property, and the buildings themselves have been demolished). On the main trail, you’ll pass a large Douglas-fir, incense cedars, and Jeffrey pine above the property and notice some old pipes used for irrigation. In short order, you’ll arrive at Morris Rodgers Creek , where the king post truss bridge was closed for safety reasons in 2023. You can ford the creek a little upstream at a small pool between the mossy rocks.

Little brook trilliums bloom along the trail here in spring. The trail has been hacked out of the rock face, and you’ll switchback steeply up to a craggy high point to get some views up and down Copper Canyon . The best views downriver are about 50 yards past the high point. Then, just beyond, is the 2021 slide, which cannot be crossed safely.
Trip Report 1 Title: Lower Rogue River/South Fork Coquille River 3-30 - 3-31-23
Trip Report 1 Text: Lower Rogue River Trail As recommended in Sullivan’s Oregon Coast guidebook, I hiked this 11½ mile trail from the east end - except that it’s no longer a through trail due to a trail-demolishing slide in Copper Canyon. It’s a very squirrelly beginning, since the hamlet of Agness, at the east end of the trail, is a bastion of private property. You have to park on the lawn at the little local library, which is also a tiny public park. Then the Forest Service directs you down a paved then a gravel road to two diversions around private homes. It’s been many years since this trail, the poor sister to the popular route between Grave Creek and Illahe, has received any maintenance. The leaf litter lies thick on the tread, cows have churned up the section above Allen Bar, and the bridge is closed at Morris Rodgers Creek. There’s a scramble diversion down to the shore of the Rogue at a cluster of downed trees and soon you hit the big cobbled bar at Allen Pool. Cattle had been grazing here on the grassy benches although I didn’t see any. Taking the trail up from the bar, I had to cut my way through a downed myrtle tree. You have to hike a short stint on a private road in order to cross Blue Jay Creek. At Smithers Creek, there’s a diversion to a couple of benches displaying the remains of a homestead, complete with irrigation pipes and fruit trees. (I checked, and this inholding may now be national forest land.) Past Morris Rodgers Creek, the trail ascends the steep slopes above Copper Canyon to a craggy viewpoint. Just west of the high point is the slide, where the rock face is sheared off, completely taking out the trail bench. Any attempt to cross without a stout pair of wings, or perhaps carabiners and bolts, would result in a lonely death. Myrtle Tree Trail A short, switchbacking trail leads up through a leafy myrtle wood to the world’s largest myrtle, also known as California bay laurel (perhaps a more accurate moniker since these trees are in the laurel, not the myrtle, family - but in this state we call them “Oregon” myrtle with pride). I love the spicy bay leaf scent of myrtle leaves when crushed between the fingers. Full disclosure, however - they release umbellulone, a chemical which is supposed to induce severe headaches, although I have never experienced that side effect (the bay leaves used in cooking don’t cause headaches as they are from another species that does not produce umbellulone). The myrtles are blooming their waxy little flowers right now. The “big” tree at the end of the trail has lost much of its height due to natural misfortunes, but its mossy but hollow lignotuber, from which new growth can sprout for centuries, is 13 feet in diameter with a 42-foot circumference. Francis Shrader Old Growth This is another short trail on the opposite side of the Rogue from the Myrtle Tree Trail. There were plenty of brochures in the box at the trailhead, and the loop takes you among old-growth Douglas-firs and Port Orford cedars (also known as Lawson’s cypress - yes, like our other “cedars”, it’s really a cypress). Some of the larger Port Orford specimens in the forest have perished from infection by a plant pathogen, Phytophthora lateralis , which may have originated in Asia. There are others which seem to be thriving, however. The interpretive loop ends at the Laddie Gale Tall Fir, named after an Oregon Ducks basketball star who led the team to the first ever national college championship in 1939. The team was known as the Tall Firs. Lauren “Laddie” Gale was born on Oakridge, but lived his later years in Gold Beach, at the mouth of the Rogue River. The Laddie Gale Douglas-fir is about 10 feet in diameter and 220 feet tall. Elk Creek Falls A very short trail leads to a viewing of a couple of the tiers of Elk Creek Falls on Elk Creek (how many Elk Creeks are there??), a tributary of the South Fork Coquille River. These damp days have also given birth to numerous attractive seasonal waterfalls. The trail takes you to the lower tier of 83 feet, and another tier is visible above that. Apparently, there are two higher tiers, producing a total drop of 190 feet. Record Port Orford Cedar From the same trailhead on FR 33 (south of Powers), the Big Tree Trail leads you switchbacking up a ridge. Recent storms have crushed many members of the local myrtle population, and I had to crawl though some of the debris. The trail reaches a ridge crest where big Douglas-firs and Port Orford cedars can be encountered. From a junction, it’s a short trot down to a grove of trees and shrubs which are botanically labeled. At the end of the trail, a viewpoint whose railings have all collapsed looks across at the mossy trunk of the world’s largest Port Orford cedar. The tree is 12 feet in diameter and about 230 feet tall. Obviously, it’s not the biggest that ever existed. Port Orford cedars produce the most valuable timber, per foot, than any other tree in the Pacific Northwest. Sawn-off stumps that are 20 feet across are known. Coquille River Falls Eden Valley Road is mostly undrivable currently, but I managed the 1½ miles up from FR 33 because they had just cleared a mudslide and ploughed the snow as far as the trailhead. The trail is all downhill, littered with storm debris, in an old-growth slope forest of Douglas-fir and Port Orford cedar. There’s an upper and a lower falls, with the scramble route to the upper tier involving a very slippery, treacherous crossing of Drowned Out Creek. This was not something to attempt at this time of year. The 65-foot lower falls were thundering! With the alders and maples still leafless, I could get a decent view also of the upper falls (50 feet), which drop over a 150-foot wide ledge.

Description:
Washington State University's Vancouver Campus sits on the grassy hillside of Mt. Vista, offering views to Gorge prominences such as Silver Star Mountain as well as farther to Mount Hood . The trail system here, named after the university's mascot, includes the forest corridor along Mill Creek, a tributary of Salmon Creek, and a barn at the Heritage Garden. The trails are best hiked on a Sunday, when there is little activity on campus. The description below takes you on a short loop: a longer walk of 4 - 5 miles is possible in less natural environments (see the WSU Cougar Trails map ).

From the Gray Lot , head back along the road, and go right on a maintenance track towards the pond. At a trail junction, go left on a chip trail along the edge of the woods. Look to your left for a trail that goes through an old fence. Take this path into a forest remnant of western red-cedar, Douglas-fir, grand fir, white alder and big-leaf maple. The trail drops to a junction, where you go left to drop down to Mill Creek. Make a left over a footbridge on a muddy track, passing a salmon spawning sign. At a junction, proceed right and head up, stooping under a fallen cedar to reach 159th Street, another campus entrance road.

Go right on the paved sidewalk, and cross Mill Creek to head up the hillside. A narrow, muddy trail leads right into the woods above Mill Creek, but you should continue up the road to the top of the slope. Here, take a chip trail that leads back behind a sign around the edge of a large field, where you'll get a view of Mount Hood and Silver Star Mountain . At a junction, keep straight on a wide trail that passes through a fence and drops down into the woods. Reach an open field, and keep to the right side of the barn. Stay right on the trail past a sign explaining the Heritage Garden, which has plantings of heirloom fruit trees. At the next junction, a spur leads right to a sign about restoration. Return and cross Mill Creek on a footbridge. The trail heads up to a boardwalk and then rises up the steep bank above the creek. A spur leads right to the Butterfly Meadow. On the main trail, pass a cable gate and go left up the slope past a pond with its resident flock of mallards to approach the Gray Lot .

Walk south on the trail with the Gray Lot to your right. Enter a narrow corridor of vegetation and keep left to pass above a residence before crossing a road and entering a large open field. The path swings right (uphill)  into a copse of trees. Turn right at a junction to exit the trees and reach another junction. Here keep right to walk from a cul-de-sac at a small parking area along a paved road. You'll pass through another line of trees to reach the Gray Lot .

Hike Name: Crack in the Ground Loop Hike
Description:
Crack in the Ground is styled a “volcanic fissure”, one that’s about two miles long and composed of 700,000-year-old lavas from Green Mountain to the north. Recent research, however, suggests that the crack was formed only about 14,000 years ago, contemporaneous with the Four Craters Lava Field, which the crack touches on its north end. The theory is that the weight of the Four Craters lavas created a shallow depression, and Crack in the Ground formed when its older lava separated along a fault line. One piece of evidence that supports this is that the western wall of the crack is higher than the eastern wall which tilts slightly towards the depression.

Most people only venture into the main (and deepest part) of the fissure. Other sections of the fissure are separated by fill, including a lot of Mount Mazama ash, but can also be explored with varying degrees of difficulty.

The signed trail leaves the parking area and passes a kiosk and a juniper-shaded bench. This is a very open steppe landscape of sagebrush, rabbitbrush, and juniper. In spring, yellow daisy, woolly groundsel, white layia, and buckwheat bloom among the sagebrush bushes. You’ll pass along a lava ridge on your left and then head up a low rise. From here, you can get glimpses of the cinder cones in the Four Craters Lava Field to the north. You may not notice it, but you’ll pass the junction for the return loop before reaching a picnic table at Crack in the Ground .

A very segmented, scramble section of the fissure heads north from here about 660 yards to reach the Four Craters Lava Field. Most people, however, do only the 360-yard section to the south. This is also the deepest section, about 70 feet below the surface. In summer, temperatures in this part of the fissure can be 20° below the surface temperature. The lava walls encase you immediately as you walk on a soft floor of Mount Mazama ash. You’ll pass a graffiti wall, thankfully the only major defacement you’ll see. There will be rock step ups and step downs also. Plants you wouldn’t see in the desert above, such as miner’s lettuce, nettle, and sedges, flourish down here. Where the fissure seems to split, keep right to squeeze through a jumble of boulders, and then pass under a collapsed side wall. You’ll pop out of this first foray at a bench under a juniper and get good views to the Four Craters. From here, you can go right to return to your vehicle by looping back on a trail that runs through the sagebrush.

It’s worth continuing south along Crack in the Ground a little longer, however. The next section of fissure involves more scrambling, and may be best viewed from its western rim. This section is sheer-sided and narrow, with several blocks of lava jammed in the opening. When you reach the third section, you can follow it through doing some scrambling. There are a couple of free-standing boulder columns before a steep rise out to the fourth section of the fissure .

This section has an active prairie falcon next, so if you’re here in the spring and early summer, it’s best not to enter. You can follow the rim a little way to see the uric acid stains showing where the nest is. Don’t be surprised if an angry mother dive-bombs you! Farther south, over the next mile, Crack in the Ground becomes much more shallow and is split into many short, consecutive segments that may not be worth your time.

Return to the exit from the first section, and find a user path leading west into the sagebrush landscape. This path winds among the sagebrush and juniper before veering right to join with the main trail about 100 yards from the picnic table at Crack in the Ground . Turn left to return to your vehicle.
Trip Report 1 Text: I spent a couple of days visiting (mostly revisiting) various spots south of Bend and out to Christmas Valley. Christmas Valley Sand Dunes This is the largest area of inland dunes in the Pacific Northwest. I did a loop from near East Camp across a few dunes and then back along the foot of the dunes. ATVs are permitted to run here, but none were about when I was slogging through the sand. Out of the dunes, I was buzzed by irate ferruginous hawks defending their nest. Eventually they settled in separate trees and gave me the gory eyeball until I left their territory. A western kingbird watched the commotion with some amusement. Sand Rock (Lost Forest) I scrambled up and around this formation in the Lost Forest. Sand Rock is tuff, not sand, and is honeycombed by chemical weathering. From the top there are views to the Newberry Caldera, the Cascades, Hager Mountain, and Yamsay Mountain. I crossed over the rock and skittered down a sand slide to loop back to my car through a portion of the Lost Forest. These ponderosas survive on half the amount of normal rainfall by reaching through the porous sandy soil to underground water trapped on the impervious bed of an ancient lake. Crack in the Ground The whole crack is about two miles long and can be explored in various segments, some necessitating short scrambles. It was created when the neighboring Four Craters lava flow, through its sheer weight, depressed the land, causing it to separate slightly and form a fissure at the western edge of the depression. A trail leads from the parking area to the first, and deepest, section. It’s like being in a cave, markedly cooler than the surrounding steppe, and plants like nettles and sedges survive down there. After the first segment, I hiked above or entered other segments. At the fourth section, I was greeted by a raucous chirping, obviously hungry chicks badgering a parent. I decided to back out of the crack to give them some space, and hiked along the rim until I saw the telltale white splashes of uric acid. I never saw the chicks, which were tucked under a rock overhang, but mom soon emerged, soared high in the sky, and then came at me in a steep dive. It was a female prairie falcon, and I could feel her slipstream as she buzzed me. After a few passes like that, she settled on a juniper and awaited my withdrawal. Derrick Cave Fail The old way to Derrick Cave and the northeast corner of the Devil’s Garden was from the Fort Rock Guard Station. Using old information, I went this way first (the way I had gone in the past), and soon encountered a closed road sign at a cattle grid. Derrick Cave Road runs across private land at this point, and the owners have closed it off. The current way to go is from Fort Rock, using Cabin Lake Road and then a series of forest roads north of the Devil’s Garden. I got just past Cabin Lake and reached a barricade. The Forest Service has closed off a whole stretch of forest until 2026 for “fire mitigation” and restoration. Hole in the Ground This is a volcanic crater west of Fort Rock reached by a rough forest road. I parked on the rim and walked down into the crater, which is a mile wide, via an ATV track. There’s a ponderosa wood on the southern slope of the crater and a few ponderosas studded about the wide open crater floor. The crater was created by violent eruptions when magma reached reservoirs of groundwater, thus initiating a steam explosion. On the sagebrush floor of the crater, various wildflowers were blooming. I took a very steep ATV track up the other side and circled back around the rim. Lava Butte I hit this one in the late afternoon, Usually, you have to take a shuttle bus from the Lava Lands Visitor Center, but I was able to drive up. There’s a very active lookout at the summit and a half mile trail around the cinder cone. The volcano produced the flow of black lava that oozed three miles to the Deschutes, damming it for several hundred years and creating Benham Falls. The Molten Land This interpretive trail leads out of the visitor center, with signs along the way explaining the volcanics and other natural history. Basically, you’re walking across Lava Butte’s contorted landscape of back lava where sagebrush lizards bask and a few plants, such as ponderosa pines, penstemon, lupine, and buckwheat, survive in small pockets of soil. The trail ends at a viewpoint towards the central Cascades. Paulina Creek Falls I went up to Paulina Lake and was hoping to explore features on the caldera rim, but there was still three feet of snow not far from the lake, so I walked both sides of Paulina Creek Falls. The creek braids above the drop, so this is a twin falls. About 2,000 years ago, an eruption tripped a massive flood which, at 10,000 cubic feet per second, gouged the creek’s gorge in just a few hours. There are three viewpoints here: one at the base of the falls, another up top on the south side of the gorge, and a third on the opposite rim. While crossing the road bridge, I encountered a dark creature making its way to the lake. I only got a fuzzy photo, but the otter had recently been in a fight and had some exposed wounds on its head, including a missing ear. McKay Crossing Falls I began this hike at the western end of the Peter Skene Ogden Trail and followed Paulina Creek up to the falls, which are just below McKay Crossing Campground. The creek tumbles and splashes its course through the ponderosa woods, with a narrow strip of verdancy next to the water.
Trip Report 2 Text: Above: Hole-in-the-Ground, August, 1969 Above: July, 2020 Back in 1969 I took a few days off work and explored Central Oregon, visiting Hole-in–the-Ground and Fort Rock. Crack-in-the-ground was on my list but the effects of the five miles of extremely rocky and dusty dirt roads leading to it deterred me. The 8 miles it would have taken to get to Crack-in–the-Ground would have been horrible. (1950’s cars didn’t have very good weather sealing!) Thus, a return trip has been in order for over 50 years and last weekend the stars aligned just right so off we went. (Hager Mt. and Yamsay Mt. were also on the list for this past weekend but we had to scratch both – more on that later.) Hole in the ground is much easier to find now with the advent of GPS, I don’t remember how long it took to find in ’69 but I remember it wasn’t easy given the maze of dirt roads in the area and the total lack of signage. The road into the Hole: A few pics: A different road back out: It was 93 degrees when we started our hike and the dust down there is the consistency of talc and anywhere from a couple inches to over six inches deep. We both needed a complete change of clothes after the hike! The real adventure started on the way back to the highway when I got a flat. Boy, is that a miserable job in 93 degree heat and in inches of dust! I’d never had a flat in this car so was very happy to find everything I needed was there and the spare had air in it. Note to self – don’t take a car with low profile tires on rocky roads. Picture of wussy spare. The next morning, the spare was flat. Bummer! That took care of our plans for Yamsay Mt. and Hager Mt. as I didn’t dare tackle any more back roads with my almost-a-tire excuse for a spare. The lone tire shop guy in Christmas Valley came to the motel and aired up the spare enough to drive to their shop. He only wanted $12.50 for the service truck call and fixing the spare and advised me not to drive to Crack-in-the-Ground on such a tire. When I gave the guy a 50 and said keep the change he offered me his brand new ATV to make the trip!! What a deal! A few photos of Crack in the Ground. All-in-all we only managed 7.5 miles of hiking on the trip but it was a trip to remember forever. dn PS: A real treat was the sunrise view from the motel the day we started back:

Description:
The Salem Hills, also known as the Red Clay Hills, form a small range in the southern part of the City of Salem. Suburban development has spread over the hills and abuts expansive rural properties. There are few trails here, but the Salem Area Trail Alliance is busy remedying the problem. The two trails described in this entry were developed in the new millennium. In 2006, work began on long-established user trails along an abandoned route of Croisan Scenic Way. In 2012, under the vision of Mark Wigg, the Trail Alliance and the Chemeketans worked together to develop the Croisan Meadow Loop in the Skyline Natural Area next to Sprague High School. From the Meadow Loop, you can also access Croisan Creek, which is out of sight for most of this hike. The two trail systems described can be connected via a short walk on city streets.

The Skyline Natural Area Trailhead is on the grounds of Charles A. Sprague High School, and the parking area may be fully occupied during the week. Use this trailhead in the summer and on weekends only, and begin at the Croisan Scenic Trailhead on weekdays at other times of the year (The route is described below to begin at the Skyline Natural Area Trailhead ).

From the Skyline Natural Area Trailhead , hike past the Salem Area Trail Alliance sign to a junction. Make a left to begin the lower section of the Croisan Meadow Loop. Douglas-fir is the dominant conifer here, with snowberry, trailing blackberry, sword fern, hazel, and invasive ivy in the understory. Poison oak can be seen climbing the tree trunks in a few places. At a junction, keep right and make a gently descending traverse under oak trees. Cross a trickling creek, and descend to the left on a spur that takes you out to a sloping meadow. Below you, Croisan Creek cuts through a layer of 15 million-year-old lava. You can take a user trail down the grassy sward to the creek itself. Tall big-leaf maples and cottonwoods shade the stream. Return to the main trail, and continue to the left. Keep left at another junction, and drop along an old road bed. Traverse up, and make two short switchbacks. Keep left at the north end of the loop, and arrive at the grounds of Sprague High School under a spreading oak tree.

If you’re continuing to the Croisan Scenic Trail, walk a short distance to a gate at Joseph Street. Go one block out along Joseph to Croisan Scenic Way, and turn left. It’s three blocks north on Croisan, crossing Joplin Street and Brock Loop, to the Croisan Scenic Trailhead .

With private homes on both sides, the trail drops off a gravel road bed to the left under a canopy of oaks. Enter a leafy big-leaf maple/Douglas-fir wood. Where the trail splits, keep left under a bower of hazel, and pass through a thimbleberry thicket. Keep left at the junction with Tony’s Trail, and stay left again to cross a footbridge, where you might spot a bat box on a tree. Head down to the left at the next junction, and cross a trickling brook to join an abandoned section of Croisan Scenic Way. This paved road is now shaded by a leafy maple bower. Stay on this road to pass a steep spur leading up to Cloudview Drive. The foot trail resumes at a grate and crosses North Croisan Creek in an ivy-carpeted forest. Keep left at all junctions (You’ll be returning to the first of these to loop up to Belcrest Memorial Park ). The trail levels in a grove of hazel and cherry trees and terminates at a grassy patch on Spring Street.

To complete the northern loop, return through the hazel/cherry grove, and take the second trail on the left. Go left again before you reach the abandoned section of Croisan Scenic Way. Pass a twenty-trunk maple, adult suckers that sprang from a large stump! Make a fairly level traverse through a carpet of ivy. Head into a lush gully, and keep left to cross North Croisan Creek to hike up a slope of ivy-draped oaks and Douglas-firs. At a junction, make a right to head steeply up to reach Belcrest Memorial Park (Keeping left at the junction would take you to the Dogwood Drive Trailhead).

You can walk up through the tranquil setting of the cemetery to the crest of the hill and then go right to exit through the brick gate and reach Browning Avenue. Make a right on Browning to pass Kurth Street and Cloud Drive. Browning veers left to crest a rise and drops to cross Cloudview Drive. A spur of the Croisan Scenic Trail takes you back into the woods at the end of Browning Avenue.

Keep left at two junctions, and cross the bat box footbridge on the main trail. At the next junction with Tony’s Trail, go left and head up the slope. Hike uphill, and then drop downhill to the right at another junction. Reach the main trail again, and go left to return to the Croisan Scenic Trailhead .

Return to Sprague High School the way you had come along Croisan Scenic Way and Joseph Street. Take the Croisan Meadow Trail into the Skyline Natural Area. Descend the slope a little from the spreading oak and, at the first junction, go left. The upper section of the loop makes an undulating traverse under Douglas-firs and big-leaf maples. There are also a few madrones reaching for the canopy. The trail drops a little to two junctions, where you need to keep left. Ascend again, and step over a few log jumps constructed by bikers. At the last junction, go left to the parking area at the Skyline Natural Area Trailhead .

The 9,000-acre Cummins Creek Wilderness has only one official trail that goes through it: this is the is Cummins Ridge Trail which bisects the wilderness by running the ridge crest along its spine. However, if you want to see the some of the massive old-growth spruce for which the wilderness is known, you’ll have to take another route. The official trails on the loop described here stay out of the wilderness, but an unofficial trail which begins near the trailhead will take you down into the wilderness and Cummins Creek itself. There are big Sitka Spruce down here as well as soggy bogs and confusing elk trails! To make a longer day out of it, continue on the regular trails. There’s one viewpoint, but also many more massive old-growth trees (Douglas-fir and western hemlock in addition to Sitka spruce), especially as you descend the Gwynn Creek Trail.

The trail follows the road bed of FR 1050 in secondary western hemlock/Sitka spruce woodland with an understory of salal, sword fern, and evergreen huckleberry. Dip to cross a trickling creek, and notice side trails to some campsites on the right. About 350 yards from the trailhead, reach the unsigned Cummins Creek-Cummins Creek Wilderness Trail Junction , and go right down this trail.

The trail, lined with deer fern and also on an old road bed, almost immediately enters the Cummins Creek Wilderness although there is no sign to inform you of the fact. Descend the slope, and cross a small creek to reach a junction. A short trail leads right to your first encounter with Cummins Creek: before reaching the bottomland itself, look for a more indistinct trail leading left – this will be your return if you’re doing the bushwhack loop described. You can continue up the creek on a user tread that swishes through large sword ferns and involves clambering over a fallen alder. The trail briefly becomes better defined before it turns away from the creek around a massive fallen spruce. Where the creek braids at a gravel bar, pass the Three Guardians , a trio of large Sitka spruce tightly lined up. You can continue to bushwhack past here past another fallen giant to reach an alder bottomland. Pass a big tree on your left, and reach one more massive spruce lying prone. You can cut inland from here to the bottom of the slope, where you should find a longer rooty user trail that leads right above the creek bottomlands. Cross a boggy brook, and climb over fallen trees. Drop to a swampy area and now, where the tread becomes much more indistinct and intersected with numerous elk trails, make your way towards the creek. Return along the bottom of the slope: this trail will take you back to the main descent trail. Make a right, and ascend the slope to the Cummins Creek-Cummins Creek Wilderness Trail Junction .

Go right here on the Cummins Creek Trail, which rises very gradually and crosses a small stream. This is a silent old-growth woodland of Sitka spruce and hemlock. Divert from the road bed to cross another creek, and suddenly notice that the spruce have ceded dominance to Douglas-fir. Alders line the road bed as you reach a ridge crest and arrive at the Cummins Creek-Cummins Creek Loop Trail West Junction .

Go left here to leave the road bed and ascend steeply at first on a very rooty tread. The gradient becomes more gentle and the Douglas-firs become more impressive, towering over an understory of sword fern, Oregon grape, red huckleberry, evergreen huckleberry, and salal. The trail dips on the ridge crest and then rises again past a trio of tall hemlocks. You’ll also notice western red-cedar here among more massive Douglas-firs. The tread then undulates along the shady crest to burst out on a grassy slope with views across to Cummins Ridge. Traverse up this sward, and reenter an alder woodland to reach the Cummins Creek-Cummins Creek Loop Trail East Junction .

Keep left at the junction, and rise up a rubbly tread along the north side of a knoll. Come to a signpost advertising the Cummins Basin Viewpoint. Go right to follow this path behind the summit of the knoll to reach the Cummins Basin Viewpoint at an opening that affords a view down the Cummins Creek valley and out to the ocean. The end of this short trail is a few yards beyond this, but this old viewpoint has now been obscured by young Douglas-firs sprouting up.

Return to the main trail, and go right to continue up the stony tread. The trail veers right at an old alignment of the Cummins Creek Trail and ascends to the bed of a former logging road. Make a traverse under alders and through salmonberry thickets to begin a gradual descent to the Cummins Creek-Cooks Ridge Trail Junction .

One road bed leads right out to the Cooks Ridge Trailhead; you need to make a sharp left to follow a gently descending gradient. Find yourself briefly on the ridge crest, and then hike along the south side of the ridge in secondary forest. Leave the road bed at a saddle, and make an undulating traverse among old-growth trees. At a massive hemlock, you’ll come to the Cooks Ridge-Gwynn Creek Trail Junction .

Go left here to descend a lush slope forested by Douglas-fir, western hemlock, and a few cedars. Switchback, and make a long traverse that will cross numerous small creek gullies. After the first creek, pass a huge Douglas-fir; below it there is a massive Sitka Spruce. There will be more old growth Douglas-firs before you catch your first glimpses of Gwynn Creek running below. As you get closer to the coast, the large spruces begin to outnumber the Douglas-firs. Pass a state park boundary sign (you are entering Neptune State Park), and enter a thicket of salal to reach the Oregon Coast-Gwynn Creek Trail Junction .

Go left to a footbridge to get your first close view of Gwynn Creek. Cross a grassy road track that affords access to nearby Highway 101, and ascend a salal slope on a rooty trail shaded by Sitka spruce. Drop down to the Oregon Coast Cummins Creek Trailhead on FR 1050; then go left to walk the last quarter mile back to your vehicle.
Trip Report 1 Text: Heading out to the coast to get away from smoke seemed like a reasonable idea, and I ended up at Cape Perpetua.
